weather: Today in Edmonds: Overcast morning giving way to partial afternoon clearing. High 57°F, low 45°F. Light south winds 5-10 mph. No rain expected—just typical spring cloud cover. Pleasant for a walk with Piper; bring a light jacket for the evening.

openings.summary: Multiple 1.0 FTE continuing contract positions for 2026-27 school year. Requires Spanish language proficiency and collaboration with colleagues to create equitable learning environments for dual language students.
Pool posting for anticipated 2026-27 vacancies. Applications reviewed as positions become available. Covers English, History, and other secondary subjects.
Pool posting for multiple secondary vacancies in 2026-27 school year. Requires Washington State teaching certificate and ELL endorsement preferred.
Multiple high school English Language Arts positions for 2026-2027 school year. District posts specific positions between April-June and August. General applicant pool also available.
0.6 FTE long-term substitute position for grades 7-8 English Language Arts, May-June 2026. Requires appropriate Washington State certification and endorsement.
Multiple full-time high school English teacher positions available across Seattle Public Schools for 2026-27 school year. Competitive salaries and benefits.
Part or full-time secondary history teacher position for 2026-27 school year at Clyde Hill Junior High/High School. Requires OSPI fingerprint/background clearance.

career.notes: Most districts are currently in pool posting phase for 2026-27 school year, with specific positions to be posted April-June and August. Edmonds and Seattle districts show salary ranges, while others require checking specific postings as they become available. All positions require Washington State teaching certificate with appropriate endorsements.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the August 2026 list for all voice parts. Expect Tuesday 6:30-9:30 pm rehearsals at Epiphany Parish Seattle; dues $75/yr, vaccinated-only. Seattle Choral Company schedules 2025-26 auditions by appointment in July–August at Phinney Ridge Lutheran Church; dues $225/season. Choral Arts NW hosts a free Music Educator Sing-in on March 15, 2026 at First United Methodist Church (Queen Anne) — a no-audition chance to sing with the ensemble.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries: Lynnwood Library offers Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Mondays 9:30-10 am and a Little Science Lab with Imagine Children’s Museum on May 11, 2:00-2:30 pm. Edmonds Library lists Baby Storytime and Preschool Storytime weekly. Imagine Children’s Museum (Everett) has play galleries designed for ages 1-12, including a Tinker Shop and indoor sock-skating rink—check imaginecm.org for daily programs.

global_news.findings: Suspect charged with attempted assassination of Trump at Washington dinner. Cole Tomas Allen, 31, from Torrance, California, has been formally charged with attempting to assassinate President Trump during the White House Correspondents' Dinner on Saturday night. Investigators say the man wanted to kill as many high-level officials as possible. The incident has raised serious questions about security protocols at high-profile events.

King Charles III begins US state visit amid heightened tensions. The British monarch arrived in Washington for a four-day state visit, his first to the US as king. The visit comes just days after the assassination attempt on President Trump, with King Charles expected to address Congress and express sympathy over the attack. The timing has added diplomatic significance given the recent security concerns.

Iran offers to reopen Strait of Hormuz if US ends war and blockade. Iranian officials have proposed reopening the critical waterway that they've kept closed during the conflict, but only if the US agrees to end its military operations and naval blockade. This development comes as global oil prices remain volatile above $100/barrel due to the ongoing disruption of shipping through the strait.

Germany commits to massive military expansion as NATO backbone. German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier announced plans for 500 billion euros ($569 billion) in defense spending, marking a historic shift for Europe's largest economy. The move comes amid growing concerns that Europe can no longer fully depend on US defense guarantees, with Germany positioning itself as the conventional defense backbone of Europe.

Mass shooting at Nigerian football pitch kills at least 29. Gunmen attacked a group of people playing football in north-east Nigeria, according to local governor Babagana Zulum. The attack occurred in the Gwoza area of Borno state, with no immediate claim of responsibility. The incident highlights ongoing security challenges in the region affected by Boko Haram and Islamic State West Africa Province insurgencies.

UK to deny refugee protections to foreign sex offenders. The British government announced amendments to its Border Security, Asylum and Immigration Bill that will exclude foreign nationals with sex offense convictions from receiving refugee status. The move is part of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's efforts to strengthen border security and address public concerns about migration. The government also plans to introduce AI tools to help process asylum cases more efficiently.

intellectual_journals.findings: Ian Penman's essay 'I'm just a sound: Back to the Beach Boys' explores the transcendent quality of Brian Wilson's music, arguing that songs like 'Don't Talk (Put Your Head on My Shoulder)' and 'God Only Knows' create cathedral-like experiences through secular hymns that achieve spiritual uplift without sentimentality. Penman positions the Beach Boys as inheritors of rock's tension between Saturday night hedonism and Sunday morning transcendence, suggesting their music offers rare intimations of the sacred in popular culture. The essay delves into the band's complex legacy, noting how their sto [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy's essay 'The role of literature as the key to personal freedom' examines Marcel Proust's defense of reading against John Ruskin's moralizing approach. Champy traces Proust's argument that literature's value lies not in moral instruction but in preserving indelible impressions that make time tangible - allowing past sensations to remain alive through memory. The essay explores how Proust's disagreement with Ruskin's utilitarian view of reading as 'training in disinterestedness' led to his conception of books as unique communication with powerful minds, distinct from social life's c [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova's meditation 'Why You' presents the self as 'a story of why you are you' - a selective retelling of chance events from cosmic origins to present moment. Drawing on Iris Murdoch's observation that 'the self is a place of illusion,' the essay explores how attachment to identity, opinions, and absolutisms becomes absurd when viewed against the 'utter choicelessness' of variables drawn from the cosmic lottery. Popova weaves together Pascal's terror at the arbitrariness of existence with the paradox that love emerges as the substance that outweighs cosmic chance, concluding that 'love  [TRUNCATED]

The Jacobin essay 'Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys' argues that delegating judgment to AI systems threatens democratic participation by eroding independent thought capacity. Citing an MIT study showing reduced brain activity among regular chatbot users, the piece frames AI as creating 'cognitive debt' - short-term efficiency gains that gradually eliminate the cognitive capacities being outsourced. The essay connects this to Kant's 1784 'What Is Enlightenment?' warning against 'self-incurred immaturity' of letting others think for you, suggesting chatbots represent a 'tech [TRUNCATED]

Will Alden's 'Waiting for Day Zero' profiles the Iranian opposition in exile at Lake Balboa Park, Los Angeles, where the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI) gathered for an Easter Sunday picnic celebrating the potential fall of the Islamic Republic. The essay captures how Reza Pahlavi, living in the US since 1979, has positioned himself as a 'transitional leader' for Iran while maintaining support in LA's 'Tehrangeles' community. Alden documents the weekly protests at Westwood's Wilshire Federal Building that have drawn thousands, noting the complex coalition from hardline monarchists to dem [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025-2026 papers advance triadic relational ontologies: a physics thesis arguing reality emerges from a simultaneous phase-locked closure of binding, transformation and relational readout; a quantum-ontology proposal using SU(3) qutrits as ontic primitives with triadic states, operators and constraints preserving Casimir invariants; and a neurophenomenology model integrating material, mental and axiological domains through consciousness, offering testable neural signatures and AI relevance.

ai_systems.findings: 2026 is the year multi-agent systems graduate from experimental to enterprise-critical. Google’s PaperOrchestra shows the new bar: five specialized agents—literature reviewer, outline generator, figure creator, citation verifier and LaTeX formatter—collaborate to turn raw lab notes into submission-ready manuscripts in ~4,000 LLM calls, a 10× efficiency gain over human workflows. The same architecture is now standard in astrophysics: Cambridge’s Cmbagent system just won the FAIR Universe weak-lensing challenge by letting a swarm of agents iteratively design, code and calibrate cosmological pipelines, beating expert teams when humans steered the loop. Meanwhile, enterprise stacks are hardening around three coordination patterns: OpenAI’s handoff SDK (manager agent delegates to specialists), Anthropic’s parallel reasoning graphs (shared context, merged outputs) and Google’s ADK (event-sourced state with rollback). Prompt engineering has flipped: instead of coaxing step-by-step reasoning,  [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating UAP disclosure in 2026 through multiple fronts. The House Oversight Task Force, led by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, is demanding 46 classified UAP videos from the Pentagon after missed deadlines, with Luna threatening subpoenas. President Trump has directed Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to release government files on UFOs/UAPs, stating "first releases will begin very, very soon." The fiscal 2026 National Defense Authorization Act includes three key provisions: requiring the Pentagon's AARO office to brief Congress on all UAP intercepts since 2004, reviewing over-classification of UAP data, and streamlining reporting processes. Whistleblower David Grusch continues advising congressional caucuses, while Rep. Tim Burchett claims classified briefings confirm "aliens exist" and that pilots are silenced for reporting craft sightings. The proposed UAP Disclosure Act 2026 would establish a UAP Records Collection at the National Archives and mandate public disclosure of rec [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The Reopening
newyorker.dek: Inside the first night back at the Met Opera after the cyber-shutdown
newyorker.text:
At 7:03 p.m. on Monday, April 27, the chandeliers of the Metropolitan Opera House rose in their familiar pre-curtain salute, and a collective sigh—part relief, part disbelief—moved through the auditorium. It had been 41 days since the Met’s servers were encrypted by a ransomware strain that the company still refuses to name publicly. During that time, the company’s 3,400 employees were furloughed, its spring season was canceled, and the Lincoln Center plaza became an impromptu farmers’ market run by out-of-work choristers selling eggs laid by their backyard hens. (“They’re surprisingly good baristas,” one regular told me. “Perfect microfoam.”)

Tonight, however, the only eggs in evidence were on the faces of the cyber-forensics team, who had spent the afternoon running last-minute penetration tests on the rebuilt network. General manager Peter Gelb, wearing the same navy suit he’d donned for every crisis press conference since 2020, stood in the orchestra pit and addressed the crowd. “We are back,” he said, “and we are not paying the ransom.” The audience erupted; a man in the parterre shouted, “Attaboy, Peter!” and was gently escorted back to his seat.

The reopening program was a canny piece of symbolism: Verdi’s “Nabucco,” the opera that reopened the house after 9/11, performed by a cast that had learned their roles via Zoom coaching and backyard rehearsal pods. Soprano Anna Netrebko, appearing as Abigaille for the first time since 2019, arrived onstage in a gown stitched from the gold mylar emergency blankets that had insulated the orchestra pit during the blackout. (The Met’s costume shop, it turns out, is remarkably adept at upcycling disaster supplies.) Conductor Yannick Nézet-Séguin, who had spent the shutdown live-streaming silent baton workouts from his Montreal apartment, led the orchestra with the frantic energy of someone making up for lost time. During the overture, the timpani misfired—a lingering artifact of the IT team’s hurried reboot—but the musicians pressed on, and by the time the chorus launched into “Va, pensiero,” even the cyber-forensics consultants in row J were misty-eyed.

Backstage, the mood was part reunion, part triage unit. Stagehands compared scar tissue from the phishing email that had started it all (subject line: “URGENT: Updated rehearsal schedule”), while choristers traded rumors about which aria had been used to test the new firewall. (Consensus: “La donna è mobile,” because if the tenor survives, the system is secure.) In the prop room, a lone server rack sat where the golden idol of Baal once stood, its LEDs blinking like votive candles. A sign taped to the front read: “DO NOT UNPLUG—WE’RE STILL NOT SURE WHAT IT DOES.”

At intermission, Gelb held court in the Grand Tier restaurant, where the menu had been reduced to a single entrée: “Ransom Risotto,” a saffron-cream concoction whose proceeds will underwrite next season’s cybersecurity budget. Asked whether the Met had received any further threats, Gelb shrugged. “We’ve moved our archives to a blockchain vault under 30 Rock,” he said. “If they want the crown jewels, they’ll need a submarine.” Nearby, a violinist tuned her instrument to the faint hum of the new quantum-encrypted Wi-Fi. “It’s a little sharp,” she said, “but so is everything else these days.”

The final chorus of exiled Hebrews brought the audience to its feet, a standing ovation that lasted six minutes and 23 seconds—precisely the length of the ransomware note that had once replaced the Met’s homepage. As the curtain fell, the house lights dimmed to a soft amber, the color of old incandescence. Outside, Lincoln Center’s fountain was illuminated in gold and burgundy, the Met’s colors, and a small crowd gathered to watch the water dance to a broadcast of the encore. A child on her father’s shoulders asked if the opera was over. “No,” he said. “It just started again.”
